--- title: Off-Shell Supersymmetry and Fractional Superspace ---

Off-Shell Supersymmetry and Fractional Superspace

Supersymmetry beyond equations of motion, and what its apparent limits may be trying to tell us

Off-shell supersymmetry is one of the most structurally revealing ideas in modern field theory. It allows the symmetry to be expressed before one imposes the equations of motion, which makes the logic of the theory sharper and often makes deeper structure visible. For that reason, it has been a central thread running through much of my work.

At the same time, off-shell supersymmetry appears to encounter severe limitations once the number of supercharges becomes too large. Rather than treating that as a purely technical annoyance, I take it as a clue that the standard framework may be too rigid. This leads naturally to fractional superspace, in which only a proper subgroup of spacetime symmetry is manifest.

Why this matters

Much of the appeal of off-shell structure is that it clarifies what is truly built into a theory and what only emerges after imposing dynamics. When that kind of control becomes unavailable, the loss is not merely aesthetic. It can make whole classes of questions harder to formulate cleanly.

That is part of what makes the apparent upper limits on off-shell supersymmetry so interesting: they suggest not just a technical barrier, but the possible need for a different conceptual framework.

What this page will eventually cover

  • What “off-shell” means and why it matters
  • Why high-supercharge off-shell formulations seem to run into trouble
  • How fractional superspace changes the usual perspective
  • Connections to supergravity, branes, and topological ideas
